title: Life Transition Coaching: Navigating the Internal Shift with Transitional Intelligence
content:
Alice Kim, LICSW

Sometimes, there is a catalyst that starts the process of reevaluating where you are in your current season. Maybe it is a life event, a milestone, or a timely conversation. Other times, it’s a realization that your life no longer fits who you are becoming. These moments can feel both vulnerable and important.

Clinically Informed Coaching: Integrating Clinical Expertise with Life Transitions

There is a meaningful difference between Life Coaching and Clinically Informed Coaching. Coaching is designed to help you move forward with partnership and support. My background as a Licensed Independent Clinical Social Worker (LICSW) adds another layer: an understanding of human behavior, emotional patterns, stress responses, and the internal blocks that can keep you stuck.

I have years of experience applying this knowledge and translating them into skills, providing therapy to hundreds of people in their own journeys. Not only do I practice it, I also teach it to undergraduate and graduate students as an adjunct professor at Boston College. I've spent over two decades supporting individuals from diverse cultures and backgrounds across a wide range of environments, including healthcare, higher education, Big Tech, and Fortune 500 companies.

I offer Clinically Informed Coaching, which means I bring a clinical foundation built over 25+ years of experience and my role as an educator, into a future-oriented, action-focused coaching process. My approach is not just about making a plan. It is about understanding the internal process of transition so you can grow through it, not just get through it.

As a TQ (Transitional Intelligence) Certified Coach, I integrate my own lived experiences into my work as a coach. I've experienced multiple life transitions myself including immigrating as a young child, navigating multiple ethnic identities as an Asian American, career pivots, parenting and empty nesting, and entering my own second half of life. Together, these perspectives help me support you in moving through your seasons of change with clarity and a grounded understanding of transformation.

Research-Backed Approaches:

Together, we will use practical, research-backed tools to support resilience:

  • Strengths-Based: We identify the internal resources you already possess. We lead with your resilience and build on what is already working.
  • Mindfulness-Informed: Uncertainty can activate stress quickly. We develop the capacity to stay grounded and present through the ups and downs.
  • Solution-Focused: We create a path aligned with your values and your "why."
  • Action with Awareness: We ensure your next steps are thoughtful, and actionable.

What to Expect in Our Sessions

  • Exploring identity: Discovering who you are today and who you want to be in this next chapter.
  • Clarifying what matters: Identifying your core values and the life that feels aligned now.
  • Building your TQ: Identifying where you are in the transition process and building skills to navigate them.
  • Working with internal blocks: Noticing patterns like over functioning, self-doubt, or fear of uncertainty and developing new mindsets and patterns.
  • Taking thoughtful action: Translating insight into practical next steps that are realistic and sustainable.

Who is This For?

Life Transition Coaching is designed for individuals who are forward thinking, ready to take purposeful steps toward a fuller and meaningful life. You might find yourself in one of these "seasons":

  • Major Life Milestones: From graduating and marriage to becoming a parent or an empty nester, these shifts require a recalibration of your identity and your daily rhythms.
  • Career Pivots: You’ve reached a level of success in your field, but you’re wondering if there’s a new path that feels more authentic to who you are today.
  • The Transition to Retirement: Often, we plan for the finances of retirement but forget the inner work. I help you navigate the shift from "doing" to "being" and redefine your purpose.
  • Rewriting the Script: You are ready to build and take respona life that is yours and take responsibility for it rather than living a life designed by others’ expectations.

Global Access

Life’s transitions don't stop at borders. Whether you’re a recent graduate starting a first job in Seoul, a parent navigating a move to London, redefining your purpose in San Francisco, or retiring in Costa Rica, Life transition coaching meets you where you are. One of the most liberating aspects of coaching is its accessibility and availability to you regardless of where you live.

Our work happens entirely online, making Clinically Informed Life Transitions Coaching accessible from any time zone. I work with clients around the US, Europe, Asia, and beyond to navigate change with intention.

Investment and Structure

Coaching is an investment in your future self. It is a dedicated space where you are the central focus, and the goal is tangible, meaningful change. I offer structured coaching packages designed to provide the consistency and momentum needed for meaningful change.

Reach out to schedule a free 15 minute consultation. Click here for coaching packages.

Reviewed and edited by Alice Kim, LICSW with AI assistance. Photo by Alice Kim.

title: Finding your Way: A Map for Navigating Life’s Transitions
content:
Have you ever found that you just entered a major milestone— graduation, a promotion, retirement, or even the birth of a child — and instead of the expected feeling of satisfaction, you feel a sense of disorientation, or even a quiet grief? Or have you experienced living a life that looks exactly the way you planned, yet feeling like a stranger within it? Or, perhaps more commonly, life did not go the way you hoped; you experienced a curve ball and feel like you’ve lost your footing?

Whatever the catalyst, these moments share a common thread: they signal that the ground beneath you has shifted. If you find yourself overwhelmed, stuck, or simply asking, "What happened and what now?", you are likely in the middle of a life transition. One helpful distinction is understanding the difference between a change and a transition. A change is the event; a transition is the internal process.

Change vs. Transition:

Change is external and can be fast. It can be the event such as the move, the diagnosis, the layoff, the breakup, empty nesting. Transition is internal and usually slower. It asks questions like “What is important? What are my values? Who am I? Who do I want to be?” It’s the psychological integration that follows.

That distinction matters because many people assume that once the event is over, they should already feel settled. But the mind and heart often needs much longer to reorganize around a loss, expectation, new role, or identity.

The Phases of a Transition:

William Bridges offers a map to understanding life's changes. Transitions begin with an ending, move through a neutral zone, and eventually open into a new beginning. That framework helps explain why life can feel unsettled internally even after something has already changed externally. A change can happen quickly, but the internal adjustment takes more time than we anticipated. There is no set timeline for this inner process. Bruce Feiler adds another important perspective: transitions are often nonlinear. He describes major disruptions as "lifequakes"—events like illness, divorce, caregiving, job loss, or relocation that shake the structure of your life. A lifequake may happen suddenly, but the transition that follows is usually longer, messier, and less predictable than people expect.

The Ending: Letting Go

Every transition begins with some form of ending, a loss. You may be leaving behind a role, a routine, a relationship, or an identity. Even welcome changes can carry grief, anxiety, or disorientation because something familiar is ending.

The Neutral Zone: The Messy Middle

Change Image

This is the space between the "no longer" and the "not yet." It often feels foggy, unproductive, or emotionally confusing, but it is also where deeper reflection happens even when it does not feel good.

The New Beginning

Over time, a new beginning starts to take shape. You are not just managing the change; you are building new patterns, meanings, and ways of being. You integrate your past with your present, evolving sense of self.

Transitional Intelligence (TQ)

To navigate these phases effectively, it helps to build what Kari Cardinale calls "Transitional Intelligence" (TQ). Cardinale has coined the term “Transitional Intelligence” as “the capacity to perceive life’s transitions as internal opportunities for growth, to recognize the stage you are in, and to skillfully move through it with compassion, clarity, and confidence.” She describes how these skills can help you move through change with greater steadiness, self-awareness, and intention.

Instead of treating transition like a problem to solve quickly, TQ focuses on your ability to notice what is ending, tolerate uncertainty, revise old assumptions, and lean into new growth and integrate it over time. This framework is useful because transitions are not just logistical. They are also emotional, relational, cognitive, and existential.

Coaching vs. Therapy

Transitions can be navigated and supported through self-reflection, community, and time. Having a coach can also offer partnership, enhance your understanding of your experience, and assist with identifying and moving you towards your values and goals. Other transitions can benefit from extra support with a therapist if it is activating anxiety, depression, trauma responses, prolonged grief, relationship strain, or a strong sense of being emotionally stuck.

Final Thoughts

Life transitions is not just a change in circumstances. Separating change from transition gives you a more nuanced map of your experience. It helps explain why some shifts feel manageable and others feel seismic.

When you focus only on the external event, it is easy to judge yourself for still feeling unsettled. But anxiety, grief, confusion, or fatigue are often a normal part of the process. Instead of asking, "Why am I not over this yet?," a more useful question may be, "What is this transition asking me to let go of, hold on to, deal with, or learn?"

Transition is an internal process of reflection, reorganization, edits, and identity evolution. Bridges names the phases, Feiler reminds us that major shifts can arrive as nonlinear lifequakes, and MEA reframes these seasons as an opportunity for revision, wisdom, and a new sense of purpose. If you are in the middle of a major shift, uncertainty is not a sign of failure. Often, it is a sign that something important is taking shape.

Sources & Further Reading

  • William Bridges — Transitions: Making Sense of Life’s Changes
  • Bruce Feiler — Life Is in the Transitions
  • https://www.meawisdom.com/ebook/the-anatomy-of-a-transition/

Note: Reviewed and edited by Alice Kim, LICSW with AI assistance. Photographs in this post are original works by Alice Kim.
